coredata.dc:title: Synthesizes, characterization, measurements and modeling thermal conductivity and viscosity of graphene quantum dots nanofluids
coredata.dc:creator: Sedaghat, F.
Yousefi, F.
coredata.dc:description: The present study is aimed to measure the thermophysical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanoparticles that dispersed in water, ethylene glycol and water-ethylene glycol mixture (60:40) as base fluids. The presence of GQDs enhance the viscosity and thermal conductivity of nanofluids considerably. The maximum thermal conductivity enhancement reaches up 53%, 21% and 18% for the nanofluid containing 0.5% GQDs at 50 °C in water, ethylene glycol and water- ethylene glycol mixture (60:40) as base fluids, respectively. In addition, the viscosity of each solution was measured, and the results show that it increases with increasing volume fractions of GQDs nanoparticles and decreased with increasing of temperature. Besides, an artificial neural network model presents to predict the thermal conductivity and viscosity of nanofluids. The overall predicted thermal conductivities and viscosities of nanofluids are in excellent agreement with experimental data with the AAD of 1.02% and R2 = 0.99929 for thermal conductivity and AAD = 1.09%, R2 = 0.99915 for viscosity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs nano fluids
Thermal conductivity
Viscosity
Neural network

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (a) and EDX (b) analysis of graphene quantum dots.
(a) UV spectra of GQDs nanofluid and (b) UV–Vis spectra with passage of time (t/day) at volume fraction φ = 0.5%.
(a), (b) and (c) viscosity of nanofluids samples as a function of temperature in water, ethylene glycol and the mixture of water and ethylene glycol (60:40) as a base fluids

(a), (b) and (c) Comparison of the volume fraction variations of different models for viscosity of GQDs nanofluids in water, ethylene glycol and the mixture of water and ethylene glycol (60:40) as a base fluids respectively.
(a), (b) and (c). Thermal conductivity of nanofluids samples as a function of temperature in water, ethylene glycol and the mixture of water and ethylene glycol (60:40) as a base fluids respectively.

(a), (b) and (c) Comparison of the volume fraction variations of different models for thermal conductuvity of GQDs nanofluids in water, ethylene glycol and the mixture of water and ethylene glycol (60:40) as a base fluids respectively.
Evolution of training, validation, and test errors as a function of the number of training epochs during ANN training for viscosity of GQDs nanofluids.
Evolution of training, validation, and test errors as a function of the number of training epochs during ANN training for thermal conductivity of GQDs nanofluids.
Modeling ability of the optimized ANN to predicate of viscosity of GQDs nanofluids.
Modeling ability of the optimized ANN to predicate of thermal conductivity of GQDs nano fluids.
Effect of the number of hidden layer neurons on AAD %
Effect of the number of hidden layer neurons on AAD %
Comparison between thermal conductivity of three different nanofluids.
Comparison between dynamic viscosity of three different nanofluids.

head.ce:title: Synthesizes, characterization, measurements and modeling thermal conductivity and viscosity of graphene quantum dots nanofluids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Yasouj University, Yasouj 75918-74831, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study is aimed to measure the thermophysical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanoparticles that dispersed in water, ethylene glycol and water-ethylene glycol mixture (60:40) as base fluids. The presence of GQDs enhance the viscosity and thermal conductivity of nanofluids considerably. The maximum thermal conductivity enhancement reaches up 53%, 21% and 18% for the nanofluid containing 0.5% GQDs at 50 °C in water, ethylene glycol and water- ethylene glycol mixture (60:40) as base fluids, respectively. In addition, the viscosity of each solution was measured, and the results show that it increases with increasing volume fractions of GQDs nanoparticles and decreased with increasing of temperature. Besides, an artificial neural network model presents to predict the thermal conductivity and viscosity of nanofluids. The overall predicted thermal conductivities and viscosities of nanofluids are in excellent agreement with experimental data with the AAD of 1.02% and R2 = 0.99929 for thermal conductivity and AAD = 1.09%, R2 = 0.99915 for viscosity.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofluids are prepared by dispersing nanometer-sized particles, generally <100 nm, in a base fluid such as water, ethylene glycol, oil and other conventional heat transfer fluids. Addition of high thermal conductivity metallic nanoparticles (e.g., copper, aluminum, silver etc.) to the base fluid increases the thermal conductivity of such mixtures, thus enhancing their overall heat transfer capability [1]. The thermal conductivity of heat transfer fluid has a considerable effect on improving the heat transfer rate, and several studies have been reported on the thermal conductivity of nanofluids, particularly water and ethylene glycol based nanofluids. The experimental investigations on thermal conductivity of the nanofluids showed a considerable enhancement compared to the base fluid [2]. Lee et al. [3] measured the thermal conductivity of four oxide nanofluids (A12O3 in water, A12O3 in ethylene glycol, CuO in water, and CuO in ethylene glycol), and reported an enhancement of >20% in the ethylene glycol–CuO nanofluid. The dependency of the thermal conductivity of nanofluid on the base fluid was experimentally studied by Xie et al. [4] with different base fluids. Amrollahi et al. [5] examined the temperature dependence of CNT nanofluids on the thermal conductivity, and their results showed an increase in the thermal conductivity of 20% using a 4.35% mass fraction of CNT in ethylene glycol. Huminic et al. [6] measured the thermo-physical properties of FeC nanoparticles in distilled water, and the results showed that the thermal conductivity of FeC/water nanofluids increased with both weight concentrations of nanoparticles and temperature. The maximum thermal conductivity enhancement was found at 1.0% and 70 °C, respectively.

Another important factor of nanofluids is viscosity. The viscosity of nanofluids is the most important factor with respect to various forced convective heat transfer applications as it has a direct bearing on the design of the flow and heat transfer equipment. Yiamsawas et al. [7] measured viscosity of Al2O3/water and TiO2/water at high temperature and high concentrations of nanofluids. They observed that in the theoretical models, the size and shape of nanoparticles, and temperature are important factors beside the volume fraction. Nguyen et al. [8] reported that the viscosity of nanofluids increases significantly with concentration and decreases with temperature.

Masuda et al. [9] have initiated studies on the effect of Al2O3, SiO2 and TiO2 nanoparticles dispersed in water on the thermal conductivity and viscosity of the nanofluid. Tavman et al. [10] reported measurement of viscosity for SiO2–water nanofluids of (0.4–1.85) volume concentration with 12 nm particle size at 20–50 °C. Measured results showed that the viscosity increased dramatically with an increase in concentration. Chen et al. [11] measured the volume fraction and temperature effects on viscosity for multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT's) with distilled water for a temperature range of 5–65 °C. They reported that viscosity increases accordingly with nanoparticle loadings when the volume fraction is higher than 0.4 vol%. Also, relative viscosity increases significantly with temperature after 55 °C. Wang et al. [12] also measured the relative viscosity of Al2O3– water and Al2O3– ethylene glycol nanofluids and the results showed similar trend of increase of relative viscosity with increased solid volume fraction for the two nanofluids.

All the reported results showed that the viscosity of nanofluids increases with the increase in the concentration of nanoparticles, and decreases with an increase in the temperature. When the GQDs nanoparticles dispersed in base fluids the surface interaction increases. The increased surface interactions could also contribute to the increase in the viscosity of the nanofluids. On the other hand, the viscosity of all the nanofluids decreases gradually with respect to the increase in the temperature, similar to the effects seen in the base fluid.
•
The objective of this study is to investigate the thermal conductivity and viscosity of water, ethylene glycol and mixture of water/EG (60:40) based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanofluids. The primary advantage of GQDs nanofluids is that the GQDS nanoparticles inherently more chemically stable than other nanoparticles. In addition, surfactant is not used during the synthesis. Considering they can be produced by simple method and they are relatively cheap. Another advantage of GQDs is large surface area that leading to have higher surface properties such as thermal conductivity and viscosity.

As a preliminary step we synthesized GQDs nanoparticles by the chemical vapor deposition method. Then we dispersed nanoparticle in water, EG and the mixture of water and EG (60:40). The nanoparticle volume fractions studied were (0.05%, 0.1%, 0.2%, 0.3%, 0.4% and 0.5%).
We also used a neuromorphic model for predicting the thermal conductivity and viscosity of GQDs nanofluids. Finally, the results of this theory have been compared with experimental data.

In this work we used GQDs nanoparticles that synthesized experimentally. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a kind of 0 dimension material of 20 nm diameter with characteristics derived from both graphene and carbon dots (CDs). It was synthesized by the chemical vapor deposition methods using active carbon and nitric acid. Firstly 2 g active carbon was added in 300 ml nitric acid 2 M. This mixture was refluxed for 24 h. The reaction products were centrifuged and the above solution was collected and then dried in an oven at the temperature of 200 °C for >12 h. When the GQDs nanoparticle are synthesized XRD, EDX and SEM analysis were done to know the structure and morphology of the prepared samples. The ethylene glycol was purchased from E Merck. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

The SEM image of GQDs is shown in (Fig. 1 (a)). Also EDX analysis evaluate the following mediated elemental proportions 34.63% at C, 65.37% O (Fig. 1(b)). Besides, X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern for GQDs is shown in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern of GQDs shows a broad pick at 2θ = 25°. It can be attributed to (002) carbon-to‑carbon spacing of 3.46 Å.

The stability of the GQDs nanofluid has been verified by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Jusco, model V–530, Tokyo, Japan). The UV–Vis analysis of GQDs shows a strong peak at 242 nm assigned to π-π∗ transitions of aromatic CC bonds. An additional shoulder peak at 307 nm was assigned to the n-π∗ electron transition of heteroatoms (CO) existed in GQDs (Fig. 3(a)). Therefore, we recorded the UV–Vis spectra with passage of time in each two days interval for one month in this wavelength. The suspension was stored in a glass container without stirring during this period. The spectra obtained are shown in Fig. 3(b) for the nanofluids at room temperature. This figure reveals that the nanofluid of GQDs in water is stable at least for one month.
Nanofluids were fabricated via two-step method, which means dispersing GQDs nanoparticles in three different solvents as a base fluids (water, ethylene glycol and water-EG mixture (60:40)).

Eq. (1) was used to calculate the values of nanoparticle and base fluids, and required amounts of them for preparation of different volume fractions.
(1)
φ =
w ρ
np
w ρ
np
+
w ρ
bf
× 100
In this equation φ, ρ and w stand for the volume fraction, density and material weight, respectively.

After estimating the amount of nanoparticles required for preparation of GQDs nanofluid for a given volume fraction using Eq. (1), nanoparticles are mixed in the base fluid of water, ethylene glycol and water-EG mixture (60:40). In the present investigation, no surfactants were added in the GQDs nanofluids, because with the addition of surfactants the thermo physical properties of nanofluids are affected.

GQDs nanofluids of six different volume fractions are prepared for measuring the thermal conductivity and viscosity of all the nanofluids concentration considered in the present work. After the preparation of nanofluids each sample is kept in bath ultrasonic for 15 min to enhance uniform dissolution of the nanoparticles. Experimental viscosity of GQDs nanofluids was measured at temperature from 10 to 40 °C with a step of 5 °C. Thermal conductivity of nanofluids was measured at temperature from 10 to 50 °C with a step of 10 °C. Three measurements are made for each sample to ensure the repeatability and accuracy of the results. It is important to measure the thermal conductivity and viscosity of base fluid at the same temperature and using the same technique than for the nanofluid. Therefore the thermal conductivity and viscosity of pure water, ethylene glycol and the mixture of water and ethylene glycol (60:40) have been measured in the whole temperature range in this work.

Viscosity measurements were performed with Brookfield Viscometer (DV-II + Pro, Paul N. Gardner Company, USA) that connected to a temperature controlled bath (Supplied by Brookfield Engineering Laboratories, USA). It is calibrated before the conduct of experiments to ensure the accuracy and repeatability of the measurements. The uncertainly of temperature and viscosity are ±0.1 K and ±0.01 cP respectively.

Fig. 4(a), (b) and (c) shows the relationship between the viscosity of the nanofluids and the temperature at various volume fractions of GQDs. The viscosity decreases with the increment of temperatures, because of the weakening of inter-molecular and inter-particle adhesion forces. In other words when the temperature is increased the inter-molecular and inter-particle adhesion forces is weaker, therefore the viscosity of nanofluids is decreased.

Viscosity also depended on volume fraction of nanofluids. When the GQDs nanoparticles dispersed in base fluids the surface interaction increases. The increased surface interactions could also contribute to the increase in the viscosity of the nanofluids.

The results also showed that the enhancement of dynamic viscosity at lower volume concentrations (0.05%, 0.1%and 0.2%) is less, while it is very significant at higher volume concentrations (0.4% and 0.5%). Moreover, the relative viscosity revealed that when the solid volume fraction enhances from 0.05% to 0.5%, the dynamic viscosity increases up to 119.30%, 152%, 128% for water, ethylene glycol and mixture of water and ethylene glycol respectively. The comparison between viscosity of three different nanofluids are collected in Table 1 .

There are some existing theoretical equations to estimate the particle suspension viscosities. One of these formulas was suggested by Einstein [13]. He presented the formula for the viscosity of fluids containing dilute, spherical, suspended particles and low volume fractions (<0.02). Einstein, model are shown in Eq. (2)
(2)
μ nf
=
μ bf
1 + 2.5 φ
where μ
nf is the viscosity of suspension; μ
bf is the viscosity of base fluid and φ is volume fraction of nanofluids.

In 1957 Brinkman [14] presented the formula for the viscosity. This equation was derived from the pioneering model of Einstein. For the suspensions with moderate particle volume fraction (<0.04) the Brinkman's formula is useful and this equation is more acceptable among the researchers.
(3)
μ nf
=
μ bf
1 − φ
− 2.5

Later, Batchelor [15] proposed the following formula in order to consider the effect of Brownian motion of nanoparticles and the interaction between them on the viscosity of nanofluids.
(4)
μ nf
=
μ bf
1 + 2.5 φ + 6.5
φ 2
It should be mentioned that most of the theoretical models depends only on the volume fraction of nanoparticles and viscosity of base fluids.

Besides, the ANN was compared to experimental data and other theoretical models, the predicted results of the ANN versus volume fraction for GQDs nanofluids with the experimental data and other above mentioned models are shown in Fig. 5(a), (b) and (c). It can be seen from these figures the ANN is has good agreement with the experimental results in contrast from other theoretical models.

Thermal conductivity was determined by a transient hot-wire method, which is one of the most accurate techniques to determine the thermal conductivity of fluids, including nanofluids, as pointed out by several authors [16–20]. Thermal conductivity data were measured using the Decagon Devices KD2 Pro Thermal Properties Analyser (Decagon Devices Inc.; USA), whose principle of measurement is based on the transient hot-wire source approach. The KD2 Pro has a probe of 60 mm length, 1.3 mm diameter with an integrated heating element, a thermo-resistor, and a microprocessor for measuring the thermal conductivity. The nanofluid sample is filled in the small container and the probe is inserted at the center of the container. The uncertainty of temperature and thermal conductivity are ±0.1 K ± 0.05
W
m . K
, respectively.

The effect of temperature on the thermal conductivity of GQDs nanofluids is shown in Fig. 6(a), (b) and (c).The temperature range is 10 °C to 50 °C. It can be clearly seen that the thermal conductivity of GQDs nanofluids increase with the temperature increasing. All thermal conductivity of the nanofluids was found to be higher than its base fluid. It is known that the enhancement of the thermal conductivity of nanofluids depends on several factors, such as the Brownian motion [21], the effects of nanoparticle aggregation [22], the interfacial layers of liquid to the liquid/particle interface [23–25], and also the combined effect of these factors [21]. It is suggested that the change in the quantity of thermal conductivity of base fluids with temperature is because of increase in intensity of Brownian motion. When the particles move randomly due to Brownian motion, they pull the nearby fluids with themselves and increase the transport of heat in the suspension. When temperature is increased, the Brownian motion increases, therefore the thermal conductivity of nanofluids is increase. Additionally, large surface – weight ratio enhances the Brownian motion.

Thermal conductivity also depended on the volume fraction of nanofluids. A substantial increase in the thermal conductivity is observed with the increase in the GQDs volume fraction for all the nanofluids. The comparison between thermal conductivity of three different nanofluids are collected in Table 2 .

Some theoretical models are existed to calculate the thermal conductivity of nanofluids. The most useful models are the Maxwell [26], Hamilton-Crosser [27], and the Lu-Lin models [28]. The Maxwell model [26] is shown in Eq. (5) that used to estimate the thermal conductivity of solid particles suspended in base fluids.
(5)
k nf
=
k p
+ 2
k bf
+ 2
k p
−
k bf
k p
+ 2
k bf
−
k p
−
k bf
φ
k bf
where, k
p , k
bf , k
nf and φ are thermal conductivity of particle, thermal conductivity of based fluid, thermal conductivity of nanofluid and volume fraction. In this equation the shape and size of nanoparticles and temperature do not considered.

The Hamilton and Crosser model [27] is more completed model than Maxwell model. Because they considered the influence of particles, shape in their equation.
(6)
k nf
=
k p
+
n − 1
k bf
−
n − 1
φ
k bf
−
k p
k p
+
n − 1
k bf
φ
k bf
−
k p
k bf
where φ the volume fraction and n is a particle shape factor (the particle sphericity) which are 1 and 0.5 for spherical shape and cylindrical shape, respectively.

Lu-Lin [28] considered only the influence of volume fraction on thermal conductivity and proposed the simple model as follows:
(7)
k nf
=
1 + 2.5 φ + 2.27
φ 2
k bf

In this study, the experimental data was compared with the ANN and above mentioned models (Fig. 7 (a), (b) and (c)). As it is shown the ANN model could well predict the effect of the volume fraction variations on thermal conductivity of GQDs nanofluids in various temperatures.

Artificial neural network (ANN) is pattern of data processing that is made by imitating human brain neural network. The artificial neural networks consist of structure of artificial neurons that have been connected in the network [29]. The ANN which is composed of fully connected processing unit (i.e., neurons) in various layers can be employed as a useful tool for function approximation, pattern recognition and is a powerful technique for data processing [30]. One kind of feed forward neural network that has been used commonly for the approximate functions is multilayer percepetron (MLP) [31–33]. The MLP consists of three or more layers (an input and an output layer with one or more hidden layers) of nonlinearly-activating nodes making it a deep neural network. Since MLPs are fully connected, each node in one layer connects with a certain weight to every node in the following layer. The input layer receives the data, the output layer delivers the answer corresponding to the property values and the hidden layer processes and organizes the information received from the input layer and delivers it to the output layer [34]. Fig. 8 shows the schematic presentation of the MLP model. Many researchers applied different types of the ANN to predict the thermophysical properties of nanofluids because of numerous applications of them in various fields [35–43].

In this work the input nodes (variables) for the ANN model are the temperature (T), volume fraction (φ), molecular weight (MW) of base fluid and the thermal conductivity and viscosity of base fluid and the output nodes (variable) are the thermal conductivity and viscosity of GQDs Nanofluids. All experimental data point of thermal conductivity that used in this study is 90. The MLP is trained, validated, and tested with random 70% (62 data points), 15% (14 data points) and 15% (14 data points) of all experimental data point, respectively. All experimental data point of viscosity that used in this work is 126. The MLP is trained, validated, and tested with random 70% (88 data points), 15% (19 data points) and 15% (19 data points) of all experimental data point, respectively.

The mathematical meaning of the errors criteria including absolute average deviation (AAD %), mean square error (MSE) and correlation coefficient (R2) values are given as bellow:
(8)
AAD =
∑
i = 1
N
X i
exp .
−
X i
cal .
/
X i
exp .
× 100
N
(9)
MSE =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
X i
exp .
−
X i
cal .
2
(10)
R 2
=
∑
i = 1
N
X i
exp .
−
X ¯
2
−
∑
i = 1
N
X i
exp .
−
X cal
¯
2
∑
i = 1
N
X i
exp .
−
X ¯
2
where N, X
i
exp. and X
i
cal. are the number of data, experimental and calculated data, respectively.

For viscosity in the first step of the training procedure, all data points were scaled to the range of [0.1, 0.5] as follow:
(11)
Output =
input − min
input
max
input
− min
input
× 0.4 + 0.1

In this study, the mean square error (MSE) was selected as a measure of the performance of the net. In general for optimizing the network, achieving generalization of the model and avoiding overtraining, we started with two neurons in the hidden layer and gradually increased the number of neurons until no significant improvement in performance of the network is observed.

The net with on hidden layer (15 neuron) with the mean square error of 6.9 × 10−6 for viscosity and 7.27 × 10−5 for thermal conductivity leads to the best prediction. Figs. 9 and 10
show the evolution of training, validation, and test errors as a function of the number of training epochs (based on early stopping approach). The analogy between predictive data and experimental data is curried out and shown in Fig. 11 . The results of Fig. 11 evaluates good compromise between the predicted data and the experimental data of viscosity of GQDs nanofluids with absolute average error, AAD = 1.09% and high correlation coefficients, R2 = 0.9986. The analogy between predictive data and experimental data of thermal conductivity is curried out and shown in Fig. 12 . The results of Fig. 12 evaluates good compromise between the predicted data and the experimental data of viscosity of GQDs nanofluids with absolute average error, AAD = 1.02% and high correlation coefficients, R2 = 0.99929. Also we shows the Effect of the number of hidden layer neurons on AAD % in Figs. 13 and 14
for viscosity and thermal conductivity of GQDs nano fluids respectively.
The neural network programming was provided in tool box of MATLAB software.

This paper reports the thermal conductivity and viscosity of GQDs nano fluids.
•
There are many synthesis methods to produce GQDs, but most of these methods need several steps and materials. Also these methods are costly. In this work GQDs have been prepared by simple and cheap method and we do not use any chemical surfactant during the synthesis.
•
The thermal conductivity of nanofluids were measured, and the obtained the results that the thermal conductivity of GQDs nanofluids is higher than the base fluid. In addition, the enhancement in the thermal conductivity was linearly correlate relationship with increase in concentration of nanoparticles. The maximum thermal conductivity enhancement reaches up 53%, 21% and 18% for the nano fluid containing 0.5% GQDs at 50 °C in water, ethylene glycol and water- ethylene glycol mixture (60:40) as base fluids respectively.
•
Thermal conductivity also depended on temperature. The rising of temperature would contribute to a rise of thermal conductivity of nanofluids.
•
The viscosity of GQDs nano fluids was measured. The results show that the viscosity of nanofluids increase when the volume fraction of nanofluids was increased and decreased when the temperature was increased.
•
In this study we compared the experimental data with ANN and some other theoretical models. As it is shown the ANN model could well predict the effect of the volume fraction variations on thermal conductivity of GQDs nanofluids in various temperatures.
•
Also the viscosity of GQDs nanofluids was compared with ANN and other theoretical models and show that ANN has higher accuracy and precision.
•
This work presents a neuromorphic model for predicting the thermal conductivity and viscosity of nanofluids. The overall predicted thermal conductivities and viscosities of nano fluids are in excellent agreement with experimental data with the AAD of 1.02% and R2 = 0.99929 for thermal conductivity and AAD = 1.09%, R2 = 0.99915 for viscosity.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Archives of Materials Science and Engineering
A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism
